This is where stuff starts to get a little more personal. So we went through the hardcore dedicated fat loss phase, transitioned into like a fat loss performance phase where we incorporated more activity, which I'll discuss in a little bit. And now we're into the recovery phase because people can't diet forever. So at this phase stage, a person has been dieting basically between 12 to 15 weeks. Why? Okay, long enough to make an enormous amount of progress. And now have that progress stick. And I don't like to use the term maintenance, which is why I like to use the term recovery. I feel like if you use maintenance with a client, they're like, what the hell am I paying you for? Nothing, right? So basically they just went through an exhausting diet. What do you want to recover from it? So this is the beginning of the recovery phase. And in terms of the nutrition implementation, this is what it signifies. So they've been in a deficit for going on for months now almost. And now, since they've dug this hole, we want to help them get out of it. And what we do is, since they've been seeing you every few weeks, and you have all their weight data, and you have their calorie data, and you have an idea of what their body fat may be, this is where things, like I said, become a little more personal. Where what we do is now we, with a slow but short progression, essentially over the next eight or nine weeks, we slowly increase their calories back up to what their maintenance level should be, at least about. And there's a couple of ways you can do this. You can use a fancy body fat measurement and use a predictive equation. Or you can use a pretty standard predictive equation, something like Harris Benedict, which you can Google that anywhere. I'll put a link down to it below. And basically you put their age, height, weight, and sex in, and just even if they're super active, you just leave their activity as whatever the minimum is. And then that is their presumed maintenance. It gives us something to shoot towards anyway, okay? And so you keep the protein static, they're about 0.8 grams per pound. If they've lost a tremendous amount of fat, then again, you might need to decrease their protein. That's cool, it gives more room for carbohydrate, right? And now, based off of the sort of preferences they have, you raise their carbohydrate and fat each week, or every two weeks, depending on how much contact you have, over the next eight or nine weeks, to what their calculated maintenance should be. So to break this down, let's say the person has been eating 1,500 calories, and their maintenance is 2,500, okay? That leaves you with 1,000 calories to add back over one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine weeks, or eight weeks, depending on where you start to incorporate this. And that means each week, you're going to be adding just over 100 calories, okay? And that will be mostly in the form of carbohydrate and a little bit of fat. And so if you have 100 calories to add back, that could be 25 carbohydrates straight up, or it could be 20 carbohydrates and a couple of grams of fat. That is something that I need to lean on you as opposed to decide by taking the preferences of the client, okay? And making sure you lean towards the direction of carbohydrate or fat based off of the client's preferences. So basically you can do all carbohydrate or carbohydrate and partial fat. Now, I don't recommend adding all fats back. I do recommend having at least some carbohydrates being added in there. But the important thing is that you know what their current caloric load is, you know what their estimated maintenance is, and over the next few months, going up to week 24, you slowly increase their calories on a weekly or bi-weekly basis to work them back up to what their maintenance for their new body weight should be. And yes, during this last phase, they should still be losing fat. At the very least, the way they look should change. And this is because in theory, they should be at a deficit until they hit their calculated maintenance. And so each week they should still be losing fat, even though you're feeding them more, they should just be losing fat slower and slower. And so this is language that I still suggest you use. You recover from the diet back up to their maintenance level, but you're adding calories, and as you're adding calories, they're still in a deficit, they still lose fat up until the very last week. And then at that point, you can stabilize, reassess, and then go on to another performance or another fat loss phase, depending on the client's priorities.
